revision.text:
{{Short description|Licensed red-light districts in Japanese history}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Yoshiwara circa 1872.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} in [[Tokyo]], 1872]]
{{nihongo||遊廓|'''Yūkaku'''}} were legal [[red-light district]]s in [[Japan]]ese history, where both [[brothel]]s and [[prostitution|prostitutes]] - known collectively as {{nihongo3|lit. "woman of pleasure"|遊女|yūjo}}, the higher ranks of which were known as {{nihongo||[[wikt:花魁|花魁]]|[[oiran]]}} - recognised by the Japanese government operated.<ref>[遊郭]『大百科事典. 第25巻』 (平凡社, 1939)</ref> Though prostitution was, officially, legal to engage in and pay for only in these areas, there were a number of places where prostitutes and brothels operated illegally, known as {{nihongo||[[:ja:岡場所|岡場所]]|Okabasho}}, the generic name for all unlicensed red-light districts other than [[Yoshiwara]] (later including both [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] and [[Shinmachi]]).<ref name="平田109">平田（1997）109頁。</ref>
In January 1946, [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] issued an order (SCAPIN 642) nationwide to abolish Japan's legalised system of prostitution, with brothels in the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} areas having to change their names to either that of {{nihongo|[[café]]|カフェ}} or {{nihongo||料亭|[[ryōtei]]}}, with the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} being renamed as {{nihongo3|lit. "red line"|赤線|[[akasen]]}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Asobi (ancient Japan)|Asobi]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Akasen]]}} * [[Yoshiwara]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

In January 1946, [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] issued an order (SCAPIN 642) nationwide to abolish Japan's legalised system of prostitution, with brothels in the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} areas having to change their names to either that of {{nihongo|[[café]]|カフェ}} or {{nihongo||料亭|[[ryōtei]]}}, with the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} being renamed as {{nihongo3|lit. "red line"|赤線|[[akasen]]}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.

{{Short description|Licensed red-light districts in Japanese history}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Yoshiwara circa 1872.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} in [[Tokyo]], 1872]]
{{nihongo||遊廓|'''Yūkaku'''}} were legal [[red-light district]]s in [[Japan]]ese history, where both [[brothel]]s and [[prostitution|prostitutes]] - known collectively as {{nihongo3|lit. "woman of pleasure"|遊女|yūjo}}, the higher ranks of which were known as {{nihongo||[[wikt:花魁|花魁]]|[[oiran]]}} - recognised by the Japanese government operated.<ref>[遊郭]『大百科事典. 第25巻』 (平凡社, 1939)</ref> Though prostitution was, officially, legal to engage in and pay for only in these areas, there were a number of places where prostitutes and brothels operated illegally, known as {{nihongo||[[:ja:岡場所|岡場所]]|Okabasho}}, the generic name for all unlicensed red-light districts other than [[Yoshiwara]] (later including both [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] and [[Shinmachi]]).<ref name="平田109">平田（1997）109頁。</ref>
In January 1946, [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] issued an order (SCAPIN 642) nationwide to abolish Japan's legalised system of prostitution, with brothels in the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} areas having to change their names to either that of {{nihongo|[[café]]|カフェ}} or {{nihongo||料亭|[[ryōtei]]}}, with the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} being renamed as {{nihongo3|lit. "red line"|赤線|[[akasen]]}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Asobi (ancient Japan)|Asobi]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Akasen]]}} * [[Yoshiwara]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

{{Short description|Licensed red-light districts in Japanese history}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Yoshiwara circa 1872.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} in [[Tokyo]], 1872]]
{{nihongo||遊廓|'''Yūkaku'''}} were legal [[red-light district]]s in [[Japan]]ese history, where both [[brothel]]s and [[prostitution|prostitutes]] - known collectively as {{nihongo3|lit. "woman of pleasure"|遊女|yūjo}}, the higher ranks of which were known as {{nihongo||[[wikt:花魁|花魁]]|[[oiran]]}} - recognised by the Japanese government operated.<ref>[遊郭]『大百科事典. 第25巻』 (平凡社, 1939)</ref> Though prostitution was, officially, legal to engage in and pay for only in these areas, there were a number of places where prostitutes and brothels operated illegally, known as {{nihongo||[[:ja:岡場所|岡場所]]|Okabasho}}, the generic name for all unlicensed red-light districts other than [[Yoshiwara]] (later including both [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] and [[Shinmachi]]).<ref name="平田109">平田（1997）109頁。</ref>
In January 1946, [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] issued an order (SCAPIN 642) nationwide to abolish Japan's legalised system of prostitution, with brothels in the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} areas having to change their names to either that of {{nihongo|[[café]]|カフェ}} or {{nihongo||料亭|[[ryōtei]]}}, with the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} being renamed as {{nihongo3|lit. "red line"|赤線|[[akasen]]}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[Asobi (ancient Japan)|Asobi]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[Akasen]]}} * [[Yoshiwara]]
== References == {{Reflist}}

"woman of pleasure"|遊女|yūjo}}, the higher ranks of which were known as {{nihongo||[[wikt:花魁|花魁]]|[[oiran]]}} - recognised by the Japanese government operated.<ref>[遊郭]『大百科事典. 第25巻』 (平凡社, 1939)</ref> Though prostitution was, officially, legal to engage in and pay for only in these areas, there were a number of places where prostitutes and brothels operated illegally, known as {{nihongo||[[:ja:岡場所|岡場所]]|Okabasho}}, the generic name for all unlicensed red-light districts other than [[Yoshiwara]] (later including both [[Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] and [[Shinmachi]]).<ref name="平田109">平田（1997）109頁。</ref>

"red line"|赤線|[[akasen]]}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.

In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.

Following this, the {{nihongo|[[:en:Anti-Prostitution Law|Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[:en:Prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.

"red line"|赤線|[[:en:akasen|akasen]]}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[:en:Anti-Prostitution Law|Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[:en:Prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.

revision.comment: Bot: reorganize categories (alphabetical, deduplicated, moved to bottom)
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1060846]] Wikidata name: yūkaku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Licensed red-light districts in Japanese history}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Yoshiwara circa 1872.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], 1872]]
{{nihongo||遊廓|'''Yūkaku'''}} were legal [[:en:Red-light district|Red-light district]]s in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese history, where both [[:en:Brothel|Brothel]]s and [[:en:Prostitution|prostitutes]] - known collectively as {{nihongo3|lit. "woman of pleasure"|遊女|yūjo}}, the higher Ranks of which were known as {{nihongo|||[[:en:Oiran|Oiran]]}} - recognised by the Japanese government operated.<ref>[遊郭]『大百科事典. 第25巻』 (平凡社, 1939)</ref> Though prostitution was, officially, legal to engage in and pay for only in these areas, there were a number of places where prostitutes and brothels operated illegally, known as {{nihongo||[[:ja:岡場所|岡場所]]|Okabasho}}, the generic name for all unlicensed red-light districts other than [[:en:Yoshiwara|Yoshiwara]] (later including both [[:en:Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] and [[:en:Shinmachi|Shinmachi]]).<ref name="平田109">平田（1997）109頁。</ref>
In January 1946, [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] issued an order (SCAPIN 642) nationwide to abolish Japan's legalised system of prostitution, with brothels in the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} areas having to change their names to either that of {{nihongo|[[:en:café|café]]|カフェ}} or {{nihongo||料亭|[[:en:ryōtei|ryōtei]]}}, with the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} being renamed as {{nihongo3|lit. "red line"|赤線|[[:en:akasen|akasen]]}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[:en:Anti-Prostitution Law|Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[:en:Prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Asobi (ancient Japan)|Asobi]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[:en:akasen|akasen]]}} * [[:en:Yoshiwara|Yoshiwara]]

In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Asobi (ancient Japan)|Asobi]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[:en:akasen|akasen]]}} * [[:en:Yoshiwara|Yoshiwara]]

revision.comment: Bot: categorize pages with unclosed brackets
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1060846]] Wikidata name: yūkaku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Licensed red-light districts in Japanese history}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Yoshiwara circa 1872.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} in [[:en:Tokyo|Tokyo]], 1872]]
{{nihongo||遊廓|'''Yūkaku'''}} were legal [[:en:Red-light district|Red-light district]]s in [[:en:Japan|Japan]]ese history, where both [[:en:Brothel|Brothel]]s and [[:en:Prostitution|prostitutes]] - known collectively as {{nihongo3|lit. "woman of pleasure"|遊女|yūjo}}, the higher Ranks of which were known as {{nihongo|||[[:en:Oiran|Oiran]]}} - recognised by the Japanese government operated.<ref>[遊郭]『大百科事典. 第25巻』 (平凡社, 1939)</ref> Though prostitution was, officially, legal to engage in and pay for only in these areas, there were a number of places where prostitutes and brothels operated illegally, known as {{nihongo||[[:ja:岡場所|岡場所]]|Okabasho}}, the generic name for all unlicensed red-light districts other than [[:en:Yoshiwara|Yoshiwara]] (later including both [[:en:Shimabara, Kyoto|Shimabara]] and [[:en:Shinmachi|Shinmachi]]).<ref name="平田109">平田（1997）109頁。</ref>
In January 1946, [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] issued an order (SCAPIN 642) nationwide to abolish Japan's legalised system of prostitution, with brothels in the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} areas having to change their names to either that of {{nihongo|[[:en:café|café]]|カフェ}} or {{nihongo||料亭|[[:en:ryōtei|ryōtei]]}}, with the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} being renamed as {{nihongo3|lit. "red line"|赤線|[[:en:akasen|akasen]]}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[:en:Anti-Prostitution Law|Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise [[:en:Prostitution in Japan|all forms of sex work]], the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|[[:en:Asobi (ancient Japan)|Asobi]]}} * {{transl|ja|[[:en:akasen|akasen]]}} * [[:en:Yoshiwara|Yoshiwara]]
== References == {{Reflist}}
{{Prostitution in Japan}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] ]] {{Japan-hist-stub}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ar:يوكاكو]][[en:Yūkaku]][[es:Yūkaku]][[fa:یوکاکو]][[ff:Yūkaku]][[hy:Յուկակու]][[id:Yūkaku]][[ja:遊廓]][[ko:유곽]][[pl:Yūkaku]][[ru:Юкаку]][[sq:Yūkaku]][[uk:Юкаку]][[zh:遊廓]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q1060846]] Wikidata name: yūkaku [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:35, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Short description|Licensed red-light districts in Japanese history}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Yoshiwara circa 1872.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo}}, 1872]]
{{nihongo||遊廓|'''Yūkaku'''}} were legal {{ill|Red-light district|en|Red-light district|WD=Q272888|lt=Red-light district}}s in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}ese history, where both {{ill|Brothel|en|Brothel|WD=Q131295|lt=Brothel}}s and {{ill|Prostitution|en|Prostitution|WD=Q36633|lt=prostitutes}} - known collectively as {{nihongo3|lit. "woman of pleasure"|遊女|yūjo}}, the higher Ranks of which were known as {{nihongo|||{{ill|Oiran|en|Oiran|WD=Q570749|lt=Oiran}}}} - recognised by the Japanese government operated.<ref>[遊郭]『大百科事典. 第25巻』 (平凡社, 1939)</ref> Though prostitution was, officially, legal to engage in and pay for only in these areas, there were a number of places where prostitutes and brothels operated illegally, known as {{nihongo||[[:ja:岡場所|岡場所]]|Okabasho}}, the generic name for all unlicensed red-light districts other than {{ill|Yoshiwara|en|Yoshiwara|WD=Q1859550|lt=Yoshiwara}} (later including both {{ill|Shimabara, Kyoto|en|Shimabara, Kyoto|WD=Q840969|lt=Shimabara}} and {{ill|Shinmachi|en|Shinmachi|WD=Q7497659|lt=Shinmachi}}).<ref name="平田109">平田（1997）109頁。</ref>
In January 1946, [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] issued an order (SCAPIN 642) nationwide to abolish Japan's legalised system of prostitution, with brothels in the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} areas having to change their names to either that of {{nihongo|[[:en:café|café]]|カフェ}} or {{nihongo||料亭|{{ill|ryōtei|en|ryōtei|WD=Q2178435|lt=ryōtei}}}}, with the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} being renamed as {{nihongo3|lit. "red line"|赤線|{{ill|akasen|en|akasen|WD=Q4700325|lt=akasen}}}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[:en:Anti-Prostitution Law|Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise {{ill|Prostitution in Japan|en|Prostitution in Japan|WD=Q1133317|lt=all forms of sex work}}, the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|{{ill|Asobi (ancient Japan)|en|Asobi (ancient Japan)|WD=Q110661378|lt=Asobi}}}} * {{transl|ja|{{ill|akasen|en|akasen|WD=Q4700325|lt=akasen}}}} * {{ill|Yoshiwara|en|Yoshiwara|WD=Q1859550|lt=Yoshiwara}}
== References == {{Reflist}}
{{Prostitution in Japan}} {{Authority control}} ‎]] ]] {{Japan-hist-stub}}

{{Short description|Licensed red-light districts in Japanese history}} {{Italic title}} [[File:Yoshiwara circa 1872.JPG|thumb|right|A {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} in {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|WD=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, 1872]]
{{nihongo||遊廓|'''Yūkaku'''}} were legal {{ill|Red-light district|en|Red-light district|WD=Q272888|lt=Red-light district|1=Red-light district}}s in {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}ese history, where both {{ill|Brothel|en|Brothel|WD=Q131295|lt=Brothel|1=Brothel}}s and {{ill|Prostitution|en|Prostitution|WD=Q36633|lt=prostitutes|1=Prostitution}} - known collectively as {{nihongo3|lit. "woman of pleasure"|遊女|yūjo}}, the higher Ranks of which were known as {{nihongo|||{{ill|Oiran|en|Oiran|WD=Q570749|lt=Oiran|1=Oiran}}}} - recognised by the Japanese government operated.<ref>[遊郭]『大百科事典. 第25巻』 (平凡社, 1939)</ref> Though prostitution was, officially, legal to engage in and pay for only in these areas, there were a number of places where prostitutes and brothels operated illegally, known as {{nihongo||[[:ja:岡場所|岡場所]]|Okabasho}}, the generic name for all unlicensed red-light districts other than {{ill|Yoshiwara|en|Yoshiwara|WD=Q1859550|lt=Yoshiwara|1=Yoshiwara}} (later including both {{ill|Shimabara, Kyoto|en|Shimabara, Kyoto|WD=Q840969|lt=Shimabara|1=Shimabara, Kyoto}} and {{ill|Shinmachi|en|Shinmachi|WD=Q7497659|lt=Shinmachi|1=Shinmachi}}).<ref name="平田109">平田（1997）109頁。</ref>
In January 1946, [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] issued an order (SCAPIN 642) nationwide to abolish Japan's legalised system of prostitution, with brothels in the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} areas having to change their names to either that of {{nihongo|[[:en:café|café]]|カフェ}} or {{nihongo||料亭|{{ill|ryōtei|en|ryōtei|WD=Q2178435|lt=ryōtei|1=Ryōtei}}}}, with the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}} being renamed as {{nihongo3|lit. "red line"|赤線|{{ill|akasen|en|akasen|WD=Q4700325|lt=akasen|1=Akasen}}}} districts.
Following this, the {{nihongo|[[:en:Anti-Prostitution Law|Anti-Prostitution Law]]|売春防止法|Baishun Bōshi Hō}} was passed in 1956, before being fully enforced two years later in 1958; though the law did not criminalise {{ill|Prostitution in Japan|en|Prostitution in Japan|WD=Q1133317|lt=all forms of sex work|1=Prostitution in Japan}}, the sale of sex with "unspecified" (meaning in this context 'unacquainted') persons was outlawed, leading to the {{transl|ja|yūkaku}}, and later {{transl|ja|akasen}}, areas to cease to exist as they once had. In spite of this, the legal sale of some sex acts and services continues to this day within Japan, circumventing the Anti-Prostitution Law either through the sale of acts that do not involve penetrative sex, or through the sale of sex only to customers who have first been 'acquainted' with prostitutes.
== See also == * {{transl|ja|{{ill|Asobi (ancient Japan)|en|Asobi (ancient Japan)|WD=Q110661378|lt=Asobi|1=Asobi (ancient Japan)}}}} * {{transl|ja|{{ill|akasen|en|akasen|WD=Q4700325|lt=akasen|1=Akasen}}}} * {{ill|Yoshiwara|en|Yoshiwara|WD=Q1859550|lt=Yoshiwara|1=Yoshiwara}}
